key_insight: Elastic energy reduction can drive abnormal grain growth in ultrafine-grained Ni thin films, selectively favoring grains with in-plane <100> alignment under cyclic loading.
engineering_relevance: This suggests grain stability in thin-film metals can fail even under macroscopic elastic deformation, which matters for durability and lifetime prediction in MEMS, coatings, and microelectronic structures.
method: Phase-field simulations to model grain growth kinetics under elastic loading.
Micromechanical analysis to identify stress- and geometry-dependent growth pathways.
Comparison across systems with different elastic anisotropy to generalize the mechanism.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports phase-field results that reproduce AGG evolution kinetics and support elastic energy reduction as a thermodynamically plausible driving force.
watch_item: The mechanism is demonstrated in simulation and micromechanical analysis, so experimental validation across broader material systems and loading histories is still needed.
paper_title: Phase-field modeling of elastically driven abnormal grain growth
